overview: The war reached toward its most dangerous target yet. On an eleventh consecutive night of strikes, President Trump threatened to hit 'Pickaxe Mountain' — an underground Iranian nuclear complex near Natanz, buried an estimated 100 metres inside rock — 'very heavily,' reopening the war's nuclear dimension just as IAEA inspectors remain locked out. The escalation overshadowed US-mediated ceasefire talks under way in Pakistan. At home the bill came due: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the war has cost $37.5bn so far and asked for roughly $88bn more, drawing bipartisan fury — one senator told him 'you, sir, are the failure.' Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned an Iranian death toll in Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent.'

keyPoints.text: On an 11th straight night of strikes, Trump threatened to hit 'Pickaxe Mountain,' an Iranian nuclear site near Natanz buried ~100m underground, 'very heavily' — reviving the war's nuclear dimension.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the war has cost $37.5bn and asked for ~$88bn more ($67bn to restock arms), facing bipartisan fury; Sen. Gary Peters told him 'you, sir, are the failure.'
The 11th night of attacks overshadowed US-mediated ceasefire talks in Pakistan aimed at salvaging the collapsed interim deal; Hegseth would give no timeline for the war's end.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned that an Iranian death toll in the Strait of Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent,' as the chokepoint stayed a live shooting zone and Brent held above $90.
Iran struck Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again after the US bombings; the Houthis extended their blockade to Bab el-Mandeb, and tankers carrying Saudi crude began turning back.
Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi described being pulled from the rubble of the leadership compound whose day-one strike killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, and has stayed on the move since.

watch.text: Whether Trump follows through on striking 'Pickaxe Mountain' — a hardened, deeply buried nuclear complex whose bombing would cross into the war's nuclear dimension and carry radiological and proliferation risk.

Whether the Senate grants Hegseth's ~$88bn request or attaches conditions — the appropriations vote that hands a Congress already hunting for an exit its first concrete lever over a war it never authorized.
Whether the US-mediated ceasefire talks in Pakistan gain any traction against an 11th night of strikes, or are overtaken by the nuclear escalation and the widening Red Sea front.

stories.title: Trump threatens to strike 'Pickaxe Mountain' on 11th night of attacks
stories.summary: President Trump threatened to attack an underground Iranian nuclear facility 'very heavily,' the BBC and Guardian reported, ahead of the eleventh consecutive night of US strikes — a threat that pulls the war back toward the nuclear file it had partly moved past. The target is the complex known in the West as 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang), sited near the Natanz enrichment plant and estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, making it far harder to destroy than surface sites already hit. Little is publicly known about the facility, but analysts have long assessed it as a deeply protected part of Iran's nuclear infrastructure. The threat lands with IAEA safeguards access suspended since earlier strikes on Natanz and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments — meaning no inspectors are inside to gauge the consequences of any strike.
stories.explainer: Striking a hardened, deeply buried nuclear site is a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. A facility 100 metres underground is built to survive conventional bombs, so hitting it 'very heavily' implies the largest bunker-busting ordnance and raises the prospect of either failure or an uncontrolled release near sensitive nuclear material. With inspectors locked out, neither side — nor the outside world — would have an independent reading of what a strike actually did to Iran's most protected nuclear infrastructure, which is precisely what makes the threat so destabilising.

stories.title: Hegseth tells Senate the war has cost $37.5bn — and asks for $88bn more
stories.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate on Tuesday that the US-Israel war on Iran has cost $37.5bn so far and asked the appropriations committee for nearly $88bn more to sustain it — including roughly $67bn to restock depleted munitions — the Guardian, Reuters and PBS reported. The request is the concrete form of the funding crunch first reported a day earlier, and it drew open bipartisan hostility: Senator Gary Peters, the Michigan Democrat, told Hegseth 'you, sir, are the failure' over the lack of a coherent Iran strategy, while Hegseth, joined by Joint Chiefs chairman Gen. Dan Caine, insisted Iran had been 'weakened' but refused to give any timeline for the war's end. The hearing turned an abstract cost into a live vote, forcing a Congress already searching for an exit to decide whether to keep paying for a campaign it never authorized.
stories.explainer: Wars run on appropriated dollars, and once the Pentagon must return to Congress mid-war for tens of billions more, the legislature acquires a financial choke-point it otherwise lacks. The $67bn to 'restock arms' is telling: it means the US has been firing off munitions faster than it can replace them, a material limit on how long an air campaign can be sustained. By pairing a war-powers push with control of the purse, lawmakers now hold two levers at once — and Hegseth's refusal to name an end date is exactly what hardens their resolve to attach conditions or refuse.

stories.title: Strikes overshadow ceasefire talks in Pakistan
stories.summary: US-mediated efforts to salvage the collapsed interim ceasefire moved to Pakistan even as the eleventh consecutive night of US strikes overshadowed them, AP and the Guardian reported. Mediators are pressing a specific proposal — earlier framed as a 10-day ceasefire — to pause the fighting long enough for both sides to step back from the chokepoints, but the diplomacy is running against a hardening war: Hegseth would set no timeline for its end, Iran has called off its interim-deal commitments, and Trump is threatening Iran's nuclear sites. Iran's government says diplomatic contacts continue through intermediaries, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio has signaled openness to talks, leaving negotiation the war's thinnest — and still only — off-ramp.
stories.explainer: With the interim deal dead and IAEA inspectors locked out, there is no standing framework to fall back on, so any de-escalation must be rebuilt from scratch through third parties — the role Oman, Qatar and now Pakistan are playing. Moving the venue to Islamabad signals the mediators are still trying, but talks that proceed under an 11th night of bombing and a fresh nuclear threat have little leverage: each strike raises the price of being seen to concede, narrowing the very opening the mediators are working.

stories.title: Rubio warns Iran toll in Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent'
stories.summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned that an Iranian death toll in the Strait of Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent,' Al Jazeera reported, a comment that points to mounting Iranian casualties in the waterway where the US is striking to force shipping open and Iran is attacking tankers to keep it shut. The strait — through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves — remained a live shooting zone through the day, and Brent crude for September delivery held above $90 a barrel, its highest of the war, with US pump prices past $4 a gallon. Rubio's framing, cast as concern over escalation, also underscores how far the confrontation over one chokepoint has drifted from a contained naval standoff toward a battle both sides are absorbing in blood.
stories.explainer: A strait where vessels are being hit and abandoned is one insurers and shippers avoid regardless of any official status, so the economic damage compounds with every ship struck — and there is no clean way to reopen a waterway both sides are shooting across. Rubio's 'dangerous precedent' language matters because it hints the US expects, and is bracing for, Iranian deaths at sea to feed the reprisal spiral: casualties in Hormuz give Tehran a fresh grievance to answer, tightening the loop that keeps oil above $90 and the strait closed.

stories.title: Iran hits Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again; Houthis widen the Red Sea front
stories.summary: Iran struck Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan again in reprisal for the US bombings, Al Jazeera reported, continuing the pattern of retaliation falling on the US-aligned Gulf states that ring Iran rather than on American soil directly. In parallel the Yemeni Houthis extended their maritime campaign: after declaring a blockade of Saudi Arabia they announced a blockade of Bab el-Mandeb — the second strait they had threatened — and Reuters reported that tankers carrying Saudi crude have begun turning back. The overlapping fronts mean the war now presses on two chokepoints and a widening ring of host states at once: Iran raising the cost of sheltering US bases, and its Houthi allies squeezing the Red Sea approaches to the region's largest oil producer.
stories.explainer: Iran fights asymmetrically, so its reprisals land on Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan — the states that host the bases — to make sheltering American forces politically and economically unbearable, while the Houthis open a second maritime front Iran need not staff itself. Bab el-Mandeb is the southern gateway to the Red Sea and Suez; blockading it alongside Hormuz threatens to reroute or halt a large share of Gulf energy exports. Tankers already turning away from Saudi crude is the first concrete sign the threat is reshaping real shipping decisions, not just insurance premiums.

stories.title: Iran's foreign minister recounts the strike that killed Khamenei
stories.summary: Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, described being pulled from the rubble of a leadership compound after the US-Israeli strike on the first day of the war — the same strike that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — and said he has kept constantly on the move since, the Guardian reported. The account is a rare first-person window into Iran's decapitation at the war's outset and how its surviving leadership has operated under threat ever since. It lands as Trump threatens Iran's nuclear sites anew, a reminder that the conflict opened with an attempt to remove Iran's top leadership and has never returned to a stable command footing.
stories.explainer: Killing a state's supreme leader on day one is a decapitation strike, and its aftershocks explain much of the war's shape: a leadership that survives by staying mobile is harder to negotiate with, slower to make binding decisions, and more reliant on hardened institutions like the IRGC. Araghchi's account matters less as news than as context — it shows why Iran's responses have been fragmented and its diplomatic signals mixed, and why any ceasefire must be struck with a leadership still operating from the rubble.

stories.title: The Pentagon's funding crunch becomes a formal ask
stories.summary: The 'quickly running short on cash' warning reported a day earlier crystallized into a formal request as Hegseth faced bipartisan scrutiny at the Senate appropriations committee, the Washington Post reported. The department put the war's cost at $37.5bn so far and sought nearly $88bn more, including about $67bn to replenish munitions burned through in eleven nights of strikes and a global naval posture. The disclosure sits atop the site's running estimate of the war's cost to US taxpayers — above $113bn as of June — and turns a budget shortfall into a live congressional decision.
stories.explainer: A mid-war funding request is a different kind of pressure than casualties or oil prices: it forces the administration to seek fresh appropriations from a Congress already searching for a way to end the war, handing lawmakers a vote — and with it the power to attach conditions or refuse — over a campaign run largely on presidential authority.

stories.title: Houthis blockade Bab el-Mandeb; Saudi tankers turn back
stories.summary: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement hardened its Red Sea campaign, announcing a blockade of Bab el-Mandeb — the second strait it had threatened — after warning that any tanker calling at a Saudi Arabian Red Sea port risks attack, Reuters and NPR reported. Reuters said tankers carrying Saudi crude have already begun turning back. The move stands as a genuine second maritime front alongside Iran's closure of Hormuz, extending the squeeze from the Persian Gulf to the Red Sea and dragging Riyadh, which has tried to stay on the sidelines, more directly into the war. President Trump has vowed the US would 'take care of' the group if it moved to block Saudi shipping.
stories.explainer: Bab el-Mandeb is the narrow southern entrance to the Red Sea and the route to the Suez Canal; controlling it lets the Houthis threaten traffic between the Gulf and Europe. Two simultaneous chokepoint blockades — Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb — is the difference between a regional disruption and a systemic squeeze on Gulf energy, and tankers already turning away from Saudi crude shows the blockade is changing shipping behavior, not merely rhetoric. Trump's vow to 'take care of' the Houthis matters because striking Yemen would formally add a third theatre to an already overstretched campaign.

stories.title: Nearly 100 US troops wounded; 17 dead across four host states
stories.summary: The Pentagon's acknowledgment that nearly 100 US service members have been wounded over two weeks of escalating strikes — disclosed a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld — continues to shape the war at home, Time and the Guardian reported. The confirmed US death toll stands at 17, spread across Iraq, Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain; the military named the Iraq fatality as Sgt Michael Swinton of Fayetteville, North Carolina, killed during a 'controlled detonation' of a downed Iranian drone, and the Wall Street Journal reported that the Iranian missile that killed two troops at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase struck US personnel housing.
stories.explainer: The injured, not just the dead, measure a war's true cost — and a count that jumped from 'dozens withheld' to nearly 100 in a day tells the public the toll has been understated. Each named casualty and each detail of a missile reaching troop housing turns an abstract tally into concrete vulnerability, feeding directly into the congressional push now colliding with Hegseth's funding request: if lawmakers conclude the scale of American losses was hidden, the case to rein the campaign in strengthens.

stories.title: 'It needs to end now': a bipartisan search for the exit
stories.summary: Democratic lawmakers are actively searching for options to halt President Trump's renewed attacks on Iran — 'It needs to end now,' with the president accused of having 'lost control of this war' — turning last week's demand to terminate the war into a hunt for a concrete legislative lever, Politico and the Guardian reported. Senator Chris Murphy, the Connecticut Democrat, argued 'Iran knows Trump will have to stop escalation before it does.' The disquiet is not confined to one party: one House Republican said 'we should have finished this a long time ago,' and the pressure sharpened at Hegseth's Senate hearing, where members of both parties challenged the war's cost and direction.
stories.explainer: Under the War Powers Resolution, Congress can force a floor vote to end US involvement in hostilities it never authorized — and 'searching for options' is the procedural step before such a vote is filed. Combined with Hegseth's ~$88bn appropriations request, the legislature is acquiring two levers at once: a war-powers vote and the power of the purse. The bind is that rising US deaths push the president to escalate while pushing Congress to quit — exactly how past American wars have ended.

stories.title: Oil holds above $90 as the shock ripples out to households
stories.summary: Brent crude for September delivery held above $90 a barrel — its highest of the war — after successive nights of US strikes and fresh tanker fires in the Strait of Hormuz, the Guardian reported. US average gasoline remains above $4 a gallon and diesel above $5, and the shock is visible in distant economies: experts warn the Reserve Bank of Australia is judged twice as likely to raise interest rates as higher fuel prices feed inflation. Even so, analysts at grist and elsewhere note the war has not yet produced a global oil crisis — spare capacity, alternative pipelines and strategic reserves have absorbed the shock — but a second contested waterway at Bab el-Mandeb shrinks that cushion.
stories.explainer: Roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through Hormuz, so each vessel actually attacked strips the market of its assumption that some crude always slips through. The $4-a-gallon pump price is the transmission belt from a foreign war to inflation and central-bank decisions worldwide, which is why crude holding above $90 is a global macro event, not just a market one — and why a second blockade at Bab el-Mandeb, forcing Saudi tankers to turn back, threatens to erode the buffers that have so far kept the shock contained.

stories.title: Iran calls off the interim deal, reviving the nuclear file
stories.summary: Tehran's formal rupture with Washington — calling off the interim peace agreement after earlier declaring the June memorandum 'suspended' — remains the backdrop to the renewed nuclear escalation, CNBC reported. With IAEA safeguards access suspended since strikes on Natanz and Iran now threatened with attacks on 'Pickaxe Mountain,' there is neither a live agreement nor independent monitoring over Iran's most sensitive facilities. Mediators are pressing a new ceasefire and Rubio has signaled openness to talks, but with US troops dead and nearly 100 wounded, the negotiating thread has frayed to near nothing.
stories.explainer: The interim deal was the last guardrail over the war's nuclear dimension: mutual commitments that, however frayed, gave both sides something to point to. Formally calling it off — not merely suspending commitments — removed that guardrail just as inspectors were locked out, which is what makes Trump's threat against a buried nuclear complex so fraught: there is no framework left to breach and no monitor left to verify the result.

stories.title: Nuclear dimension lingers at Bushehr
stories.summary: Satellite imagery published last week showed fresh damage inside Bushehr, Iran's largest nuclear power plant, from strikes dated between 7 and 12 July — the window in which the ceasefire collapsed — and Iranian officials have said US strikes also hit an under-construction nuclear plant. With IAEA safeguards access suspended and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments, outside monitors are left reliant on satellites to gauge the condition of sensitive facilities. Trump's fresh threat against the 'Pickaxe Mountain' complex near Natanz sharpens the concern that the war is edging toward a nuclear-safety crisis.
stories.explainer: Bushehr is a civilian power reactor, not an enrichment site, so the danger near it is radiological rather than proliferation: damage to a fueled reactor's cooling or containment could release contamination across the Gulf regardless of who is blamed. With inspectors locked out, satellite analysis is the only independent window into whether strikes on any Iranian nuclear facility — Bushehr or the buried complex Trump now threatens — are edging toward a safety crisis.

stories.title: Israel says 'no interest' in joining, but readies plans
stories.summary: Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — has sent a deliberately mixed message about re-entering the renewed war. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the US military campaign against Iran, Al Jazeera reported, a public signal of restraint, even as CNN reported that Israel is quietly readying plans in case it is drawn back in. Israel has stayed off the battlefield since the fighting reignited on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel, but has repeatedly warned it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian weapons strike its territory.
stories.explainer: Israel rejoining is the fastest way to widen the war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and other 'Axis of Resistance' militias along Israel's borders. Smotrich's 'no interest' and CNN's 'readying plans' are not contradictory: they describe a government that wants to stay out but is hedging against a single errant Iranian missile that could trip its 'full force' tripwire and force it back in.

stories.title: The Iran war is America's least popular since polling began
stories.summary: The renewed Iran war is the least popular US conflict since modern polling began, The Economist reported, with public support falling below even the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam wars. The finding is driving events: it underpins the search for a way to terminate the war and the hostile reception Hegseth met at the Senate as American troops die — 17, per ABC News — and pump prices climb past $4 a gallon. The unpopularity sharpens what The Guardian calls Trump's 'midterm disaster' risk with elections approaching.
stories.explainer: Domestic unpopularity is a strategic variable, not just a political one: a president fighting a war the public rejects has less room to escalate and more incentive to seek an exit — but dead soldiers pull the other way, creating pressure to 'finish' the fight rather than be seen abandoning it. That tension, now voiced openly on Capitol Hill and in the fight over Hegseth's funding request, is exactly the bind Washington faces with midterms approaching.

stories.title: Iran's reprisals keep falling on US Gulf allies
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation has continued to fall on the US-aligned states that ring it rather than on American soil directly, with fresh reprisals against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan accompanying the latest nights of strikes, the Guardian and Al Jazeera reported. Iran has separately claimed it attacked Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain — a rare claimed strike on commercial cloud-computing facilities — and in recent days a fire hit a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant. The pattern matches Iran's threat, reported by The Hill, that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. A claimed strike on cloud infrastructure widens the target set from bases and power plants to the digital backbone of the region — a way to make hosting American forces and companies politically and economically unbearable.

notes: Morning refresh of the 2026-07-22 briefing. The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this draws on the workflow-committed feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-22T07:42Z; freshest item 07:40Z). Since the 2026-07-21 22:06Z briefing the war escalated on two fronts: Trump threatened to hit the underground 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz 'very heavily' on an 11th consecutive night of strikes, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the war has cost $37.5bn so far and requested roughly $88bn more (~$67bn to restock arms), facing bipartisan hostility — Sen. Gary Peters told him 'you, sir, are the failure.' The 11th night overshadowed US-mediated ceasefire talks in Pakistan; Rubio warned an Iranian toll in Hormuz would set a 'dangerous precedent'; Iran again struck Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan; the Houthis extended their blockade to Bab el-Mandeb and Saudi tankers began turning back; and Iran's FM Araghchi recounted surviving the day-one strike that killed Supreme Leader Khamenei. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — the war's $37.5bn cost and ~$88bn ask, casualty and injury figures (17 dead, ~100 wounded), the '70%' missile-retention estimate, oil and pump prices, the depth and nature of 'Pickaxe Mountain,' and Iranian- or Houthi-announced actions — are attributed to their sources.
